behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watcher behavior across directory depth (recursive vs non-recursive semantics), plus daemon handoff from watcher to pipeline. MOCK-MISS: this is not just one function; it depends on module interaction and actual watch events. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: in-process mocked events can miss OS/library-specific recursion failures. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by behavior text "placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root" because that specifically depends on real watch coverage over the filesystem tree.
behaviors.test_description: Run the daemon against a real temporary input/output directory tree, create a nested subdirectory under the watched root, write a file into that nested path, and assert one transformed output appears without restart. Use real filesystem operations and the real watcher implementation.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Primary failure boundary is local module wiring (request intake -> dedup window/state -> enqueue pipeline) with time-window state propagation. No real OS/network/subprocess is inherently required by the behavior wording (it says "processing request," not filesystem event). Rejected higher level: System is disqualified because the behavior does not require real OS primitives; a deterministic in-process setup with controlled clock and real collaborating modules can expose dedup bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real request-ingest + dedup + queue modules together with a controlled clock, submit two processing requests for the same path inside the configured window, and assert only one pipeline execution occurs; then submit outside the window and assert processing occurs again.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly cites OS-emitted duplicate events. REAL-INFRA is required because duplication patterns are produced by real filesystem/watcher behavior. MOCK-HIDE: mocked single or synthetic duplicate events often miss real timing/coalescing edge cases that cause double-processing. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by text "operating system emits multiple filesystem events".
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon with real watcher on a temp directory, perform a write pattern known to produce multiple events on the host OS (e.g., write+rename/flush sequence), record processing invocations, and assert exactly one completed processing for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This is an OS-level lifecycle condition (directory deletion/recreation) requiring real watcher resilience and re-subscription behavior at runtime. MOCK-MISS: failure appears at boundary between OS events and daemon watcher-recovery logic. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by behavior text "directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running" which requires real filesystem state transitions.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon continuously on a real temp watched directory, delete the directory, recreate it at the same path, then place a new file and assert it is processed without daemon restart.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering correctness depends on real arrival concurrency plus filesystem timestamp behavior/granularity and watcher event timing. MOCK-HIDE: synthetic ordered inputs can hide races and timestamp resolution issues seen only with real filesystem writes. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by behavior text "arrive at the same time" and "creation timestamps," which ties correctness to real filesystem timing behavior.
behaviors.test_description: With daemon running on real directories, create multiple files near-simultaneously (including close timestamp collisions), capture processing order, and verify order matches filesystem creation-time ordering rule implemented by the daemon.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem write-in-progress behavior and event timing (partial writes, close/flush semantics). REAL-INFRA is necessary; in-process mocks often cannot faithfully reproduce open-handle/partial-content timing. Rejected lower level: Integration is disqualified by behavior text "still being written to when the arrival event fires" because correctness depends on real OS/file-write state.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with real watcher, open a file and write it in chunks with delays while keeping the handle open, verify daemon does not process mid-write, then close/finish write and verify exactly one processing occurs with complete content.
